original_hypothesis.title: SGX Instance Duplication via Missing Registration State Update
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When an SGX instance rotates its key, the new instance address is not marked as registered. This allows the enclave to re-register the same new address via the attestation flow, obtaining multiple valid instance IDs for a single key.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The _replaceInstance function fails to set addressRegistered[newInstance] = true. An attacker can use the Automata DCAP attestation for the new key to call registerInstance(), which will succeed and create a duplicate instance ID. This breaks the 1-to-1 mapping invariant and allows the enclave to juggle multiple IDs to bypass operational limits.
preconditions: Attacker controls a valid SGX enclave
Attacker can generate a valid DCAP attestation for the new key

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State variable tracking uniqueness is not updated during the lifecycle of the object

operational_questions: Can an enclave generate an attestation quote for a key it just rotated to?
Does the protocol rely on instance ID uniqueness for any off-chain monitoring or slashing?
validation_requirements: Rotate an SGX key via verifyProof
Call registerInstance with an attestation for the new key
Verify a second instance ID is created for the same address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Submit a proof that rotates the SGX key from oldInstance to newInstance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Generate a DCAP attestation quote for newInstance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: SgxVerifier creates a new instance ID for newInstance, giving the enclave two active IDs
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires custom SGX client to extract quotes

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Corruption of the SGX registry, potential bypass of instance-level rate limits or slashing
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix state tracking

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple instance IDs mapping to the same address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Rapid registration of instances immediately following a rotation

validation_result.impact_summary: After SGX key rotation via verifyProof, the new instance address is not marked in addressRegistered, allowing re-registration via registerInstance to obtain duplicate instance IDs for the same key, breaking the one-address-one-instance invariant.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _replaceInstance function in SgxVerifier.sol updates the instances mapping for the given ID but fails to set addressRegistered[newInstance] = true. The _addInstances function (called by registerInstance) relies on addressRegistered to enforce uniqueness. Since _replaceInstance never sets this flag for the newInstance address, a subsequent call to registerInstance with a valid DCAP attestation for the same address will pass the addressRegistered check and create a second valid instance ID. This directly violates the documented invariant that one address should only have one instance ID.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is clearly visible by comparing _replaceInstance (which omits addressRegistered update) with _addInstances (which correctly sets addressRegistered[_instances[i]] = true). The code comment on the addressRegistered mapping explicitly states its purpose is to prevent 'getting multiple valid instanceIds,' yet _replaceInstance bypasses this protection.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This function updates instances[id] with the newInstance address but never sets addressRegistered[newInstance] = true. After this call, addressRegistered[newInstance] remains false, allowing registerInstance to create a duplicate entry.

This function correctly checks addressRegistered before adding. However, since _replaceInstance never sets this flag for newInstance, this check will pass for addresses that were already installed via key rotation, allowing duplicate registration.

calling_sequence: 2. verifyProof parses proof data: id, newInstance, signature
4. _isInstanceValid(id, oldInstance) confirms old instance is valid
5. _replaceInstance(id, oldInstance, newInstance) updates instances[id] but does NOT set addressRegistered[newInstance] = true
6. Later, attacker calls registerInstance with DCAP attestation for newInstance
7. registerInstance calls _addInstances which checks addressRegistered[newInstance] — it's false
8. _addInstances creates a new instance entry at nextInstanceId for the same address
9. Now two instance IDs map to the same address
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: verifyProof -> _replaceInstance (missing addressRegistered[newInstance] = true) -> later registerInstance -> _addInstances passes addressRegistered check -> duplicate instance ID created

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment assumptions needed. The defect is in the core contract logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: 1. SGX enclave E is registered with instance ID=0, address A0. addressRegistered[A0]=true. 2. E proves a block, providing newInstance=A1 in proof data. verifyProof calls _replaceInstance(0, A0, A1). instances[0] = Instance(A1, now). addressRegistered[A1] is still false. 3. E generates a valid DCAP attestation quote for A1 (the enclave controls this key). 4. E calls registerInstance with the attestation. _addInstances checks addressRegistered[A1] == false, passes. Creates instances[1] = Instance(A1, now + 1 day). Sets addressRegistered[A1] = true. 5. Now A1 has two valid instance IDs (0 and 1). The enclave can use either for future proofs, effectively bypassing the expiry mechanism by rotating between them.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The vulnerability allows an SGX enclave to obtain multiple valid instance IDs for a single key, breaking the one-to-one invariant between addresses and instance IDs. This enables: (1) bypassing the 180-day expiry by maintaining multiple IDs and re-registering as needed, (2) maintaining proof capability even if one instance ID is deleted by the rollup_watchdog, and (3) potential corruption of SGX instance registry integrity relied upon by off-chain monitoring systems.
recommended_mitigations: Add addressRegistered[newInstance] = true in _replaceInstance to prevent re-registration of rotated keys
Consider also adding a check: if (addressRegistered[newInstance]) revert SGX_ALREADY_ATTESTED(); in _replaceInstance to prevent replacing with an already-registered address
Optionally clear addressRegistered[oldInstance] = false in _replaceInstance if the old key should be allowed to re-attest (though this is a design decision)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy SgxVerifier with a mock AddressManager and mock IAttestation. 2. Add an SGX instance A0 via addInstances. 3. Simulate verifyProof by calling it from the 'taiko' address, with proof data containing newInstance=A1, recovering to A0 via ECDSA. 4. Verify instances[0].addr == A1 and addressRegistered[A1] == false. 5. Mock IAttestation to return verified=true for A1. 6. Call registerInstance for A1. 7. Assert that a second instance ID was created for A1 (nextInstanceId incremented, instances[1].addr == A1). 8. Both instance IDs are now valid for the same address.
uncertainties: The practical exploitability depends on whether the attacker can generate a valid DCAP attestation for the new key, which requires control of a legitimate SGX enclave
The actual impact depends on whether the protocol enforces any per-instance-ID rate limits or monitoring that would be bypassed by having duplicate IDs
additional_testing_needed: Verify that DCAP attestation can be generated for keys produced during rotation (not just initial boot)
Test whether deleteInstances properly handles the case where multiple IDs point to the same address
Assess whether off-chain monitoring or slashing systems are affected by duplicate instance IDs
